text:
Mass spectrometry-based proteomics  is a high-powered  tool  that ena-
bles  the  qualitative  and  quantitative  characterisation  of  thousands  of 
peptides and proteins in biological samples across various experimental 
conditions.  However, the existence of several computational platforms 
for processing raw proteomics data along with their different output for-
mats  require advanced knowledge  for obtaining  final  interpretable  re-
sults. 
To address this issue several approaches have been developed. The soft-
ware Perseus (Tyanova et al., 2016) provides a graphical user interface

Other software tools are developed as R-Shiny apps for example ProVi-
sion (Gallant JL et al, 2020) a web-based proteomics data analysis plat-
form  for downstream analysis of MaxQuant output, and LFQ-Analyst 
(Shah AD et al, 2020)  which enables the automatic analysis of label-
free data of MaxQuant (Cox J et al, 2008) and MSFragger (Kong A.T et 
al, 2017) data output format. Moreover, the existence of other software 
tools such as MSstats (Choi M et al, 2014) or Tidyproteomics (Jones J 
et al, 2023) allows the automatization of many data processing steps but 
1

TraianProt is a tool created using the R Shiny package and designed with 
packages  such  as  shinywidgets,  shinydashboard  and  shinyjs  with  the 
aim of creating a user-friendly experience. The modules that constitute 
the application are illustrated in Figure 1. 
 
The  first module addresses the  file input section which  requires a table 
containing the identified proteins along with their corresponding intensity 
values as well as any additional information such as the number of pep-
tides or peptide spectrum matches (PSMs) identified. The required data

tables  include  the  proteingroups.txt  file  from  MaxQuant,  the  com-
bined_protein.tsv  file  from MSFragger,  the  output  file  from  Proteome 
Discoverer  or  Proteoscape  and  the  report.pg_matrix.tsv  file  from DIA-
NN. Additionally, for  DIA-NN output files, Traianprot extracts the num-
ber of peptides and unique peptides for each protein identified in the ex-
periment from the report.tsv file that must be located in the same direc-
tory. Additional parameters indicating the computational platform, the la-
bel type, the organism and the quantification mode are required. Intensity 
or spectral count columns are extracted using regular expressions which 
define  the  samples  for  each  condition  in  the  experiment which  sets  the 
lack of need for a metadata file. The pre-processing section allows users 
to filter out proteins by valid values per group, only proteins identified in 
a minimal number of replicates are kept for the analysis. In addition, pro-
teins can be filtered according to the number of unique peptides identified. 
Sample intensities are log2 transformed and several options are displayed 
in the Normalisation section: no normalisation, mean, median, trimMean 
and variance stabilization normalisation (vsn).  
Finally, several imputation options for missing values are displayed in the 
Imputation section: no imputation, randomized sampling from a normal 
distribution and K-Nearest-Neighbour imputation.  
The  pre-processing  step  is  reactive  allowing  changes  to  automatically 
propagate through subsequent steps in the app. The Venn Diagram section 
allows users to plot a publication-ready Venn diagram.   
The Quality control module covers a group of plots that describe the na-
ture of data, such as boxplot, Q-Q plots, histograms, pre and post-imputa-
tion plots, dispersion plots, scatter, correlation and principal component 
analysis plots all of them can be downloaded in publication-ready format 
(.tiff).  
 
The Differential analysis module enables users to perform paired or un-
paired two-sample t-tests. Users can choose between the base R Student´s 
t-test function or the t-test function approach from the limma (Ritchie ME 
et al, 2015) package. In case the limma approach is selected, a PSMs cor-
rection using the DEqMS (Zhu Y et al 2020) package can be applied to 
n. In the initial step, quantification matrices are uploaded, specifying parameters such 
ns selected for each group to enable subsequent comparisons. Within the Pre-processing 
utcomes of which can be assessed through the plots generated in the Quality Control 
with several options obtaining a list of proteins that exhibit an increase or decrease in 
e plots generated in the Differential analysis plots module, and are subsequently utilized 
ule. Finally, networks illustrating associations between differentially abundant proteins 
action analysis.  
2

TraianProt  is  an  open  source  web-based,  user-friendly  platform  de-
signed  for  proteomics  data  analysis  supporting  output  formats  from 
leading computational platforms. Its primary aim is to facilitate the ex-
traction  of  interpretable  results  from  proteomic  data  by  performing 
downstream analyses and generating high-quality, customisable graphs 
and statistics in a dynamic environment. The platform allows users to 
revisit  the analysis and adjust parameters  to achieve the most optimal 
outcomes  and makes  it  accessible  for new users by not  requiring  any 
programming knowledge. This platform  is  freely available  in  the  fol-
lowing Github repository: 
https://github.com/SamueldelaCamaraFuentes/TraianProt

achieve a more accurate variance estimation. The inherent dependence of 
protein variance on the number of PSMs or peptides used for quantifica-
tion is taken into account by this method. Users can specify a log2 fold 
change threshold a p-value or adjusted p-value threshold and whether ex-
clusive proteins (proteins exhibiting absence or presence, being quantified 
exclusively in one of the studied conditions) have to be included in the 
differentially abundant protein list. 
The statistical analysis is fully reactive as well which allows statistical 
changes to propagate to the next sections.  A resulting dataframe com-
posed  by  differentially  abundant  proteins  including  non-differentially 
abundant proteins and containing several statistical metrics such as fold 
change values, p-value/ adjusted p-value etc is displayed in a data table 
that can be exported as a csv file for further analysis.  
 
Differentially abundant proteins can be visualized as volcano plot and 
heatmap plot in the Differential analysis plot module, these plots can be 
exported  in  various  file  formats,  including  publication-ready  format 
(.tiff).  
The Functional Analysis module performs  functional enrichment anal-
ysis of differentially abundant proteins using the gprofiler2 (Raudverse 
U et al, 2019) R package.  
Users can select the organism of the experiment establishing a threshold 
for significant results. The output of gprofiler2 constituted by a data ta-
ble  containing  biological  terms  and  pathways  is  displayed  in  various 
plots including a dot plot, a bar plot of terms and a Manhattan plot. Pa-
rameters such as the number of terms to display in the plot, the font size 
and the title can be customized. These plots can be also exported in var-
ious file formats, including  publication-ready format (.tiff).  Addition-
ally,  a downloadable output  table with data  sources,  term names,     p-
values and other columns is displayed. 
The Interaction analysis module performs a protein-protein interaction 
analysis  using  the  STRINGdb  (Szklarczyk D  et  al,  2023)  R  package 
where users need to specify the NCBI taxonomy identifier of the organ-
ism on which  the  experiment has been  performed  along with  a  score 
threshold.  A  network  illustrating  both  direct  (physical)  and  indirect 
(functional) associations between differentially abundant proteins is dis-
played. One network is shown for proteins with increased abundance, 
and another for proteins with decreased abundance.   
3   Conclusion 
 
TraianProt  is  an  open  source  web-based,  user-friendly  platform  de-
signed  for  proteomics  data  analysis  supporting  output  formats  from 
leading computational platforms. Its primary aim is to facilitate the ex-
traction  of  interpretable  results  from  proteomic  data  by  performing 
downstream analyses and generating high-quality, customisable graphs 
and statistics in a dynamic environment. The platform allows users to 
revisit  the analysis and adjust parameters  to achieve the most optimal 
outcomes  and makes  it  accessible  for new users by not  requiring  any 
programming knowledge. This platform  is  freely available  in  the  fol-
lowing Github repository: 
https://github.com/SamueldelaCamaraFuentes/TraianProt
